system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — system-of-tonal-convergence

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

# The Convergence System: A Book Distillation

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

The book constructs a comprehensive harmonic method called the **Convergence System**, a framework for organizing the entire chromatic scale into directed, purposeful motion toward any tonal center. The pedagogical arc moves from philosophical foundation to technical apparatus to applied practice: Chapter 1 establishes the governing metaphor and core vocabulary; Chapter 2 builds the full 24-scale engine and its voice-leading rules; Chapter 3 introduces key individual scales and the "no wrong notes" philosophy; Chapter 4 demonstrates convergence through scale examples and mode application; and Chapters 5–8 present progressively combined forms—chord-only, scale-chord mixed, chord-on-chord—culminating in Chapter 8's treatment of the dominant seventh and minor chord as the system's central harmonic terrain. The book is therefore structured as a spiral: each layer reapplies the same convergence logic at a higher level of complexity.

## The Master's Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The system's organizing metaphor is a **360° compass**, with twenty-four scales surrounding a central key center and "bombarding" it from all harmonic directions (Chapter 3). The foundational concept is **convergence**—directional harmonic arrival at a tonic—governed by what the author calls the **tonal gravity law**, the principle that every chromatic pitch has a cadential relationship to any key center and exerts pull toward it (Chapter 1). The system was conceived at the **II-V cadence**, and the dominant seventh chord—particularly its **tritone**—is identified as the most flexible and load-bearing chord type in the system, capable of hosting variants such as 7♭9, 7♯5, and 7♯9 (Chapter 1). The **Circle of Gravities** (Chapter 8) maps tritone relationships systematically, serving as a navigational tool for chord-on-chord motion.

## The 24-Scale Engine and Voice-Leading Rules

The technical core of the book is the set of **Scale Justification Sheets** (Chapter 2), which apply 24 scales to a target chord in order to establish **tonal significance**—the harmonic weight needed to make a scale "justify" its resolution. The engine rests on two rules: first, that every chord represents a member chord of one of the modes of its parent scale; second, a **universal voice-leading rule** stipulating that the final note of every scale in the system lands a whole step or half step away from the root, third, or fifth of the target tonality, ensuring smooth resolution regardless of which scale is used. Chapter 3 specifies key individual scales by number—**Scale #11** (a descending tritone-based scale), **Scale #15** (half-diminished/half-whole-tone with leading-tone function), and **Scale #16** (major with raised 4th)—and establishes that all 24 scales can be connected in any order for convergence. The **tritone** functions throughout as the pivot interval within the 2-5 cadence (Chapter 2).

## Methodology: Ear Priming, Sound Projection, and Rotation

The system's methodology is ear-first. Before playing any scale, the practitioner is instructed to **prime the ear with cadences**, mentally hearing the target tonality before executing the approach (Chapter 2). This is coupled with **sound projection**—mentally projecting oneself to the target tonality before arriving there—and the **plunging process**, a trained ability to drop into any tonal destination from any chromatic point (Chapter 2). The characteristic compositional move is **rotating multiple scales in different orders** over a single chord, especially the dominant seventh, and deploying these convergent scales in **turnbacks, cadences, and cycles**—the structural units the author identifies as comprising all jazz (Chapter 1). Chapter 4 extends this by showing how different modes of the same key (e.g., Dorian mode and G Jazz melodic minor) can each serve as convergence scales over the same target, and how the **final tetrachord** functions as a structural bridge into resolution.

## The "No Wrong Notes" Philosophy and Governing Concept

A defining philosophical commitment runs through Chapters 3 and 4: the entire chromatic scale is available to the improviser when the five governing factors—**key center, Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, Resolution, and the artist's confidence**—are properly understood (Chapter 3). Mistakes are reframed not as failures but as generative events, "a new field, a new octave," that a prepared musician can spontaneously fill (Chapter 4). The master explicitly rejects prescriptive technique in favor of developing **consciousness and awareness** as the governing principle (Chapter 4). This philosophy is the theoretical license that allows 24 scales, drawn from across the chromatic spectrum and described with harmonic colors—major, minor, augmented, dominant, diminished—to converge on a single tonal destination without contradiction. The system teaches, ultimately, that harmonic fluency is not a matter of avoiding wrong notes but of understanding the directionality—the tonal gravity—that makes every note navigable.

# Chapter 1 — Conceiving this Convergence System (scales & their chords)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter introduces the foundational concepts of the Convergence System, which treats scales as raw material for extracting chordal sounds and organizing them by harmonic color—major, minor, augmented, dominant, and diminished functioning like a color spectrum in music. The core pedagogical objective is expanding the musician's ear awareness to the entire chromatic scale by understanding that every pitch has a cadential relationship to any key center, governed by a "tonal gravity law" that creates directional harmonic motion toward the tonic. The system was conceived at the II-V cadence, where the author discovered the wealth of chord possibilities available on the dominant (V) chord, particularly the seventh chord with its tritone, identified as the most flexible chord type hosting variations like 7b9, 7#5, and 7#9. The characteristic move is combining multiple scales (rotating them in different orders) over a single chord, especially the dominant seventh, and applying these convergent scales in turnbacks, cadences, and cycles—the structural elements that comprise all music in jazz. Key terminology includes "convergence" (directional harmonic arrival), "tonal gravity law," "scale color," and "convergent chord," with Bb7 serving as the first concrete example of a convergent chord to Eb minor and major tonalities.

# Chapter 2 — Scale Justification Sheets (24)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces the Scale Justification Sheets, a convergence system that bombards a target chord with 24 scales to establish harmonic "tonal significance." The foundational rule—that each chord represents a member chord sound of one of the modes of its parent scale—underpins the entire method. Key instructional moves include: priming the ear with cadences before playing each scale, understanding the tritone as the pivot interval within the 2-5 cadence, and extracting all possible chords from scales for resolution purposes. The system employs a universal voice-leading rule stating that the final note of every scale in the system sits only a whole or half step away from the root, third, or fifth of the target tonality, ensuring smooth resolution. Load-bearing terminology includes the "tonal significance" concept, the distinction between two diminished scale types (H-W and W-H interval structures), and the sound-projection technique (mentally projecting yourself sound-wise to the target tonality). The ultimate goal is chromatic tonal fluency—training the ear to execute a "plunging process" into any tonal destination from any chromatic point.

# Chapter 3 — Scale of the System and some Constituent Chords and their Resolutions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

This chapter establishes the harmonic foundation of the system by introducing Scale #11 (a descending tritone-based scale), Scale #15 (half-diminished / half-whole-tone with leading-tone function), and Scale #16 (major with raised 4th), along with the principle that all scales can be connected in any order for convergence into a tonal center. The author articulates a radical philosophy of "no wrong notes"—asserting that when factors like key center, Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, Resolution, and the artist's confidence are properly considered, the entire chromatic scale is at the artist's disposal. The system is organized as a 360° compass metaphor, with twenty-four scales and their constituent chords surrounding and "bombarding" a central key center in all directions. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes Scale #11, Scale #15 (and its half-diminished color and leading-tone function), Scale #16 (major with raised 4th), Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, and the geometric concept of tonal convergence.

# Chapter 4 — Scale Examples of Convergence

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches convergence through scale examples, demonstrating how the final tetrachord functions as a structural bridge and how different modes of the same key (exemplified by Dorian mode and G Jazz melodic minor) can serve as convergence scales. The master's characteristic philosophy emerges in his rejection of prescriptive technique in favor of developing consciousness and awareness, emphasizing that mistakes are not failures but generative opportunities—"a new field, a new octave"—that allow musicians to spontaneously fill moments with prepared readiness. Load-bearing terminology includes "tetrachord" as the convergence mechanism, "mode" as a tool for harmonic flexibility, "enharmonic equivalence," and the central concept of "consciousness" as the governing principle that replaces formulaic instruction.

# Chapter 8 — Convergence on the Seventh Chord, Minor Chord

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 teaches the foundational principle of chord-on-chord convergence, where main chords can be embellished or approached through secondary chords, typically a dominant-seventh chord with a tritone that can precede the target chord. The master's characteristic move is the use of chromatic tones to resolve the tritone interval inherent in dominant seventh chords, establishing a core technique for applying scales over these chords. The chapter introduces the 'Circle of Gravities' as a systematic map of tritone relationships and establishes practical rules for handling scale-key conflicts, such as using a major-3rd scale to embellish the fifth of a minor chord despite the apparent tonal mismatch. This convergence framework becomes the harmonic scaffolding for understanding how chords relate to and transition between one another in tonal music.

# Chapter 1 — Conceiving this Convergence System (scales & their chords)

_Pages 4-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Scales as raw material for chords

> Each scale may be used as raw material for convergent ideas
> within a piece of music as well as many chordal sounds extracted
> from them,

**Significance (p. 4).** This is the core methodological rule.

## Combining scales in rotas

> These scales & their chords may be combined (several of them
> together) in several rotas varying their order and making more
> interesting musical usuage of color (A,—»; +, 0, 7th).

**Significance (p. 4).** Rotating multiple scales together distinguishes advanced convergence usage.

## Goal: expanding ear to chromatic resources

> The
> important factor is the expanding of hearing tolerance increasing
> the musicians ear awareness to the entire tonal resources of the
> chromatic scale.

**Significance (p. 4).** States the pedagogical objective.

## Scales in turnbacks, cadences, cycles

> The usuage of these scales in turnbacks, cadences and cycles
> (which comprise all music) in Jazz yields a wider source of ideas
>
> for the improvisor-composer-arranger.

**Significance (p. 4).** Locates where convergence scales are applied.

## Tonal gravity law

> A tonal gravity law is created once you start to play music
> (in a key). The key of the music exerts a gravity pull on all
> other chords no matter what they may be.

**Significance (p. 4).** Defines the theoretical underpinning of convergence.

## Convergence toward key center

> The energy amassed in
> a jazz improvisors solo has to be climatically released by
> arrival "Home" to the overall key center.

**Significance (p. 4).** Defines convergence as directional motion toward a tonic.

## Bb7 as V convergent chord example

> Bb7 is a
>
> standard V convergent chord to EP minor & major tonalities as well
>
>
>
> as many other chords and their parent scales in this convergent
>
> system.

**Significance (p. 4).** First concrete harmonic example in the book.

## Chromatic scale tonal relationships

> A single note removed from the chromatic scale has a relation-
> ship to every remaining tone. If the tone represents a tonality
> or key center, all remaining intervals chords and scales have a
> certain type of converginal relationship in a cadential motion
> sense to this key center.

**Significance (p. 4).** Opening axiom: every chromatic pitch has cadential relationship to any key center.

## 7th chord as most flexible chord type

> The 7th chord having the all important tritone host the
> largest and most flexible of all the chord types. (7th bs, 7b 9,
> 7th #5, 7th #9, 7th 9s, ren bg bs ect).

**Significance (p. 4).** Defines the dominant seventh chord as the pivot of the convergence system.

## System conceived at II-V cadence

> It was at
> this final juncture (II-V) where my convergence system was conceived,
> noticing all the chord possibilities on the final 5th chord in the
> cadence.

**Significance (p. 4).** Locates the generative moment of the system at the II-V cadence.

## Scale color types as harmonic spectrum

> Major, minor, augmented, dominant & diminish became
> like the green, blue, black, red & yellow of the color spectrum
> showing a manifestation and parallelism of two art forms.

**Significance (p. 4).** Defines the color metaphor that organizes the system's taxonomy.

## Using many scale colors on one chord

> This,
> from listening to John Coltrane, Yusef Lateef and others caused
> me to try using many different scale "color" types on one chord
> (7th).

**Significance (p. 4).** Key practical rule: multiple scales over a single dominant seventh chord.

# Chapter 2 — Scale Justification Sheets (24)

_Pages 17-41._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Convergence system definition

> This convergence system shows a fixed bombardment of
> any type chord that we want to give "tonal significance" to then
> bombard it with 24 scales, a mixture of them, their ideas and
> constituent chords.

**Significance (p. 17).** Core definitional statement of the entire method.

## 2-5 cadence and tritone

> The 2-5 cadence is one of the most regulary occuring devices
> in music. It has participated in music in assuming main tonalities
> and lesser tonalities & to propel musical motion. The central in-
> terval of harmonic signficance of the 2-5 cadence has been the
> "mysterious" tritone interval.

**Significance (p. 17).** Tritone within 2-5 cadence as the pivot point.

## Chord-scale relationship rule

> Each chord represents a member chord sound of one of the modes
> of the scale it comes from.

**Significance (p. 17).** Foundational rule linking every chord to a parent scale mode.

## Ear preparation before playing scales

> Before playing each scale in this
> system, you should brainwash your ear with the
> tonality (F major in this case) with several cadences
> into the key.

**Significance (p. 18).** Concrete practice rule — prime ear with cadences before convergent scale.

## Last-note resolution rule (all 24 scales)

> The last note
>
> of all the scales in this system are only a whole
>
> or half step away from the Ist, 3rd, or 5th or root
>
> of the desired tonal destination.

**Significance (p. 22).** Universal voice-leading rule validating every scale in the system.

## Scale #4 — Diminished, interval structure

> This scale is a diminish scale on the tonic "C".
>
> It has an interval structure half, whole, half, whole,
>
> half, whole, half, whole.

**Significance (p. 21).** Defines diminished scale with precise interval formula.

## Scale #5 — Second diminished type, W-H

> This scale is a second type diminish scale. It has
>
> a W,H,W,H,W,H,W,H step symmetry. It permits ascending
> diminish color to a point of convergence (F major in
> this case).

**Significance (p. 22).** Distinguishes second diminished scale type from H-W.

## Extracting chords from scales for resolution

> Extract all possible chords from all scales for
>
> resolution purposes.

**Significance (p. 20).** Direct practice directive.

## Sound-projection technique

> When converging on a tonality project yourself sound wise to
> it already having your attention (sound attention) on it and
>
> on the chord or scale you are using.

**Significance (p. 20).** Mental aural technique.

## System goal — chromatic tonal fluency

> This whole Process is one of the ear becoming
> accustomed to hearing new harmonic & linear motion
> until it is completely familiar with a plunging
> Process into a tonal destination from any note, scale
>
> or chord in the chromatic scale (Universe).

**Significance (p. 26).** Ultimate technical goal: chromatic fluency.

## System scope — 24 scales and permutations

> Remember this system is about how to get into a major or minor
>
> key variating different scales (24) and chords (many) and their
>
> innumerable permutation & chord inversions.

**Significance (p. 23).** Concise restatement of method scope.

# Chapter 4 — Scale Examples of Convergence

_Pages 50-56._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Last tetrachord as convergence mechanism

> The last tetrachord of this scale is an
> F major scale.

**Significance (p. 50).** Final tetrachord as structural convergence mechanism.

## Dorian mode as convergence scale

> This scale is simply a dorian mode of the F major

**Significance (p. 50).** Defines Scale #20 as Dorian mode.

## Same key, different mode

> This scale is an example of being within the same
> scale of (F major) the key you are converging
>
> however starting on a different mode of it.

**Significance (p. 50).** Convergence scales can begin on any mode of the target key.

## G Jazz Melodic Minor as II convergence

> Also the G Jazz melodic minor scale has the same
>
> notes as this scale so it may be used as a II
>
> convergence scale.

**Significance (p. 50).** Enharmonic equivalence rule.

## System philosophy: consciousness over prescription

> This theoretical justification of each scale of this system has to
>
> be applied to all other tonalities in the chromatic scale. This
>
> system does not dictate to the student what to do other than in-
>
> crease his or her consciousness. You shape the raw material or
>
> resources to your desired needs.

**Significance (p. 50).** Governing rule: system is awareness framework not formula.

## Mistake as creative opportunity

> I do not believe in perfection
>
> of the moment. I do believe in a readiness or nowness to fill a
> moment in time with something spontaneously prepared at the moment
> needed. A mistake is a new field, a new octave.

**Significance (p. 50).** Performance philosophy: mistakes are generative opportunities.

# Chapter 8 — Convergence on the Seventh Chord, Minor Chord

_Pages 69-84._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chord-on-chord convergence rule

> The main chords in tunes may be converged on or embellished
> with other chords that may precede or lead up to them. Whatever
> chord you are leading up to (main chord) is or can be usually pre-
> ceded by a five chord with a tritone. This interval participates
> in this five or dominant-seventh chord as well as another seventh
> chord a flatted 5th from its root.

**Significance (p. 66).** Foundational rule of chord-on-chord convergence.

## Scale-to-7th-chord resolution rule

> It is just a matter of using chomatic tones to resolve the tritone

**Significance (p. 69).** Core technique rule for applying scales over dominant seventh chords.

## Minor key: scale with major 3rd

> Scale #3 has a major 3rd already in it so it has to used as above
> to embellish the 5th of the F minor.

**Significance (p. 70).** Practical rule for handling scale-key conflict.
